content:
Key Findings

  • Ribble Valley Borough Council paid 1,282 unique suppliers between 2021/22 and 2025/26
  • 343 suppliers received under £1,000 each, accounting for 26.7% of all suppliers
  • 589 suppliers received between £1,000 and £10,000 each, making up 45.9% of all suppliers
  • Only 6 suppliers received over £1 million each
  • The total spend analysed was £38.6 million across 13,723 transactions

The Long Tail: Ribble Valley's Hundreds of Small Suppliers

Ribble Valley Borough Council, like many local authorities, relies on a vast network of suppliers to deliver services to residents. The council's spending data reveals a long tail of suppliers, with the majority receiving relatively small amounts. This pattern raises questions about procurement efficiency and potential savings through consolidation.

Between 2021/22 and 2025/26, Ribble Valley paid out £38.6 million to 1,282 unique suppliers. This extensive supplier base includes everything from large contractors to small local businesses. The data shows that 343 suppliers received under £1,000 each, while only 6 suppliers received over £1 million each. This distribution highlights the fragmented nature of the council's procurement activities.

Supplier Size Distribution

The majority of suppliers fall into the smaller spending categories. A total of 589 suppliers received between £1,000 and £10,000 each, making up 45.9% of all suppliers. In contrast, only 6 suppliers received over £1 million each. This long tail of small suppliers could indicate a lack of consolidation in procurement, potentially leading to higher administrative costs and less leverage for negotiating better prices.

Consolidating procurement could help the council reduce administrative overheads and achieve better value for money. However, it is also important to consider the benefits of supporting local businesses and maintaining a diverse supplier base. Balancing these factors is a key challenge for the council's procurement strategy.

Potential for Consolidation

Consolidating procurement could help the council reduce administrative overheads and achieve better value for money. However, it is also important to consider the benefits of supporting local businesses and maintaining a diverse supplier base. Balancing these factors is a key challenge for the council's procurement strategy.

The council could explore opportunities to consolidate spending with fewer suppliers where appropriate, while still ensuring a competitive and diverse market. This approach could help reduce the administrative burden of managing a large number of small contracts, while still supporting local businesses.

What You Can Do

  • Submit a Freedom of Information request to Ribble Valley Borough Council about their procurement consolidation strategies
  • Attend council committee meetings to learn more about their procurement policies and practices
  • Check the council's published contracts register to see who they are awarding contracts to and for what services
  • Write to your local councillor about the importance of balancing procurement consolidation with support for local businesses
  • Visit aidoge.co.uk to explore the full spending data and see how your council is spending public money
This article is based on Ribble Valley Borough Council's published spending data covering 2021/22 to 2025/26. All figures are drawn directly from transactions published under the Local Government Transparency Code. Analysis by AI DOGE.
